title: On Making
content_html:
2026-03-12

I made this!

TLDR: I gain a lot of fulfillment by making things. I don't consider things built by others at my request to be made by me, and are therefore much less fulfilling. And then I feel sad. This article starts strong and then heads off into the weeds.

There have been a lot of pieces written about what I'll call "the AI dev schism" And I think there's a lot of truth to those:

  • Loss of the craft, coding things by hand
  • Loss of low-level problem-solving
  • Loss of fun
  • Gain of high-level problem-solving
  • Getting through back-burnered projects
  • Gain of fun

We'll just grant those as being correct for various developers. But there's something else that troubles me.

Backstory before we get going, so you can get a better idea of my perspective:

  • I'm a Gen-X hacker; I cut my teeth 80s microcomputer era.
  • I hold a BS and MS in CS.
  • I have 20 years industry experience, (Hewlett-Packard, startups, cofounder, Activision, etc.).
  • CS instructor for the last 9 years, now at Oregon State University-Cascades.
  • I'm 65% Doom on the AI-Utopia/Doom scale.
  • I'm a Claude Code user sometimes.
  • I code by hand sometimes.
  • My father taught philosophy at a community college for 35 years. This might help explain the latter part of this blog entry.

I'm going to use "AI" to mean "Generative AI and LLMs" in this essay. Sorry, veterans of so many AI winters.

Interlude!

Before we begin, I'd like to share with you a bit of my latest sci-fi novel. Some of you might unaware that, in addition to Beej's Guides, I also write science fiction.

Kael pressed his back against the shattered bulkhead, plasma scoring the air centimeters from his face. The Vorrkai assault drones had anticipated their route through the lower decks and now Rin was bleeding through her jacket sleeve and old Maret couldn't stop coughing from the vented coolant still hazing the corridor. Kael counted the pulse-intervals between shots. Three seconds. Maybe four. That was all the universe was offering him.

Then he saw it: the maintenance shaft behind the collapsed generator housing, its grate blown half-open by the same explosion that had caved in their original exit. It was tight. It was ugly. It ran directly over the Vorrkai's forward position, which was either the most dangerous path imaginable or the last one they'd ever think to watch. Kael grabbed Maret's collar and pointed without a word. The old man's eyes went wide, then hard. He nodded. Rin was already moving.

Kael came last, returning fire blind around the bulkhead corner, not to hit anything, just to make noise, and to give the drones something thermal to track while his people scrambled into the dark. A bolt caught the generator housing and the whole structure groaned, raining sparks down into the shaft on top of them. He hauled himself in, knees burning on the torn metal, and pulled the grate closed behind him with a sound he was certain every Vorrkai unit on the deck had heard. In the black ahead, Rin's hand found his wrist. Move, her grip said. Now. And so they did.

—Excerpt from The Vorrkai Interval, by Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall

And, in my now-copious spare time I make art! This is a woodcut, painted in pastels, showing some of my favorite subjects.

Mirrors of the Machine by Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall, $1300.

Carpentry? You bet I dabble! I rebuilt my front deck recently. The old one was rotting out, so I grabbed a bunch of cedar and put it together. I'd been meaning to do it for a while, but couldn't find the time.

And, finally, here's some of the code I wrote for a TUI adventure roguelike:

    fn try_move(&mut self, dx: i32, dy: i32) {
        let nx = self.player.x + dx;
        let ny = self.player.y + dy;

        // Check for monster combat
        if let Some(idx) = self.world.monster_at(nx, ny) {
            let result = {
                let monster = &mut self.world.monsters[idx];
                resolve_combat(&mut self.player, monster, &mut self.rng)
            };

            self.messages.push_many(result.messages);

            if result.monster_defeated {
                let monster = &self.world.monsters[idx];
                let xp = monster.xp_reward;
                let gold = monster.gold_reward;
                self.player.xp += xp;
                self.player.gold += gold;
                if gold > 0 {
                    self.messages.push(format!("You find {} gold!", gold));
                }
                if self.player.try_level_up() {
                    self.messages.push(format!(
                        "Level up! You are now level {}!",
                        self.player.level
                    ));
                }
            }

            if !self.player.is_alive() {
                self.messages.push("You have been slain! Rest in peace...");
            }

            self.advance_turn();
            return;
        }

        // Check terrain passability
        if self.world.is_passable(nx, ny) {
            self.player.x = nx;
            self.player.y = ny;
            self.advance_turn();
        } else {
            let terrain = self.world.terrain_at(nx, ny);
            self.messages.push(format!("The {} blocks your path.", terrain.name()));
        }
    }

I'm an extremely prolific polymath, I'm sure you'd agree!

I Am Uncomfortable

I don't like lying. And yet I feel, dear reader, I have misled you. Yes, all that has been created (including my deck) and I was the initiator of all that creation. But I don't really feel like I made any of it. I'm uncomfortable claiming that I did so.

Since you are certainly aware by now that all of the above is AI-generated (except my deck, which was created by skilled, paid craftsmen), perhaps you feel a little bit of discomfort with me claiming credit for doing those things, too.

However, I don't think everyone feels this way. I know many people who ask contractors to build things and they phrase it like they built it.

"I put in a new front deck," they'd say, even though other people did all the work. Personally, I feel that's misleading. I'm more of a "I had a new front deck put in" kind of person.

And when I do have Claude create something for me, I just can't say that I made it. Other people can, but I just can't. Again, I'm more prone to say, "I had this code built for me." I don't even feel comfortable MIT-licensing that (not-for-hire) work, if that's even legally possible. I just Unlicense it all.

As a manager, I'd never say that I built a product. "My team built this," I'd say. And as a manager of LLMs: "My Agents built this."

And that, for me, has very little weight in terms of making.

I don't feel like I did anything. And I like doing things. I find pride in doing things.

Completing projects is great. I love completing projects. Capitalists love completing projects. Real artists ship.

But having others complete projects I initiated is entirely less fulfilling to me.

It's not just the loss of the craft and the problem-solving challenge and whatever else. It's the loss of making.

What Did I Make Recently?

My wife wanted a no-frills flash card system for learning Spanish. "I just want a thing where I can put the words I want in a spreadsheet and then see it on flash cards." A prompt!

So I wrote it. By hand. I did use Claude to learn some basics, like the easiest way to get the data out of a Google Sheet (spoiler: it's the CSV endpoint), but I told it to generate no code.

--------------------------------------------
Language                     files      code
--------------------------------------------
JavaScript                       2       112
CSS                              1        33
HTML                             1        32
--------------------------------------------
SUM:                             4       177
--------------------------------------------

Didn't take long. Only about 50x longer than it would have taken Claude to do it.

But I can put my name on that code and say that I made it. Was it a lot of code? No. Was it groundbreaking and amazing? Certainly not. But I'm infinitely more proud of that code than anything I've had Claude write, because I'm not capable of being proud of the latter.

And my wife wouldn't go to her book club and say, "I wrote a flash card system to study Spanish." Admittedly, part of this would be because she didn't want to appear a geek, but mostly it's because it's untrue, even though she initiated the process.

What About The Art and Craft of Prompting?

After all, you create the prompts, don't you? You said you were proud of doing things. Isn't that doing a thing? And since so much got done, isn't it even more of doing a thing?

I don't disagree. And I do agree that there is skill here in some important ways.

  • You have to apply vision.
  • You have to apply judgment.
  • You have to apply communication skill.
  • You have to apply prompting skill.

Not all prompts are equally effective. Not all users of AI are as effective as one another. There's a very human contribution to be made here.

But the skill is in effectively asking someone to make something for you.

"Leadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it."

          —Dwight D. Eisenhower

And I'm the kind of person who really misses the making of software. And prompting for software, to me, isn't the same as making the software. It's the same as asking someone else to make it.

What About Compilers, Smartypants?

Isn't it just turtles all the way down?

For any given C or Rust program, did I write that program? I wrote it in C. I didn't write any machine code. The computer wrote that.

There's something different in my mind about that. I'm not sure if it's the mathematically-precise relationship between the C and the machine code...? And yet that's not spelled out. I'm relying on some hacker's deep knowledge of the CPU to generate that magnificent set of optimized instructions when I stupidly divide something by seven. And it's not deterministic, strictly, in that Clang and GCC emit different instructions. What about different platforms? The C is portable and the machine code is not. Did I write that program that's running on Windows even though I personally only ever built it on Linux?

Going further in the murky gray, what if I instruct an LLM to be a C compiler and emit assembly code for some platform?

Let's try:

Convert this C program into x86_64 assembly for Linux.

#include <stdio.h>

int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}

int main(void)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)
        printf("%d: %d\n", i, fib(i));
}

Claude says:

# fib.s - Recursive Fibonacci, x86_64 Linux, AT&T syntax
# Build: gcc fib.s -o fib -no-pie

    .section .rodata
fmt:
    .string "%d: %d\n"

    .section .text
    .globl main

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  int fib(int n)          n in: %edi / return: %eax  │
# │  Stack frame:                                        │
# │    %rbp-8  = saved %rbx  (via push)                 │
# │    %rbp-12 = n  (spilled so it survives both calls)  │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
fib:
    pushq   %rbp
    movq    %rsp, %rbp
    pushq   %rbx                # callee-saved scratch
    subq    $8, %rsp            # 1 local (int n); keeps stack 16-byte aligned

    movl    %edi, -12(%rbp)     # spill n to stack

    cmpl    $1, %edi
    jle     .Lbase              # n <= 1 → return n

    # --- fib(n-1) ---
    movl    -12(%rbp), %edi
    subl    $1, %edi
    call    fib
    movl    %eax, %ebx          # %ebx = fib(n-1)

    # --- fib(n-2) ---
    movl    -12(%rbp), %edi     # reload n (call clobbered %edi)
    subl    $2, %edi
    call    fib                 # %eax = fib(n-2)

    addl    %ebx, %eax          # return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
    jmp     .Lret

.Lbase:
    movl    %edi, %eax          # return n

.Lret:
    addq    $8, %rsp
    popq    %rbx
    popq    %rbp
    ret

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  int main(void)                                      │
# │  Stack frame:                                        │
# │    %rbp-4  = int i  (loop counter)                  │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
main:
    pushq   %rbp
    movq    %rsp, %rbp
    subq    $16, %rsp           # locals + alignment

    movl    $0, -4(%rbp)        # i = 0

.Lloop:
    cmpl    $10, -4(%rbp)
    jge     .Ldone              # i >= 10 → exit

    # fib(i)
    movl    -4(%rbp), %edi
    call    fib

    # printf("%d: %d\n", i, fib(i))
    movl    -4(%rbp), %esi      # arg2 = i
    movl    %eax, %edx          # arg3 = fib(i)
    leaq    fmt(%rip), %rdi     # arg1 = fmt  (RIP-relative)
    xorl    %eax, %eax          # AL=0: no XMM args to printf
    call    printf

    addl    $1, -4(%rbp)        # i++
    jmp     .Lloop

.Ldone:
    xorl    %eax, %eax          # return 0
    leave
    ret

OK, clanker, but does it work?

% gcc fib.s -o fib -no-pie
% ./fib
0: 0
1: 1
2: 1
3: 2
4: 3
5: 5
6: 8
7: 13
8: 21
9: 34

Well, holy golden ratios! It even used XOR to set exit status 0, the cheeky devil!

So did I write that program? Yes. I feel I did. (I actually did hand-code the Fibonacci thing just now—I've done it a million times for classes.) I feel I wrote the program even though I didn't write the assembly, which is the program that actually runs! The LLM wrote that!

Technically, it's the machine code that runs, but going from assembly to machine code now we're really getting into the land of strict, brainless, mathematically-precise translation, which I feel doesn't take away from the act of making any more than using a hammer to drive nails does.

What's the real difference in that gray area? I'm not sure. I think it hinges on something that has to do with making versus asking to be made, even if that line isn't particularly clear.

"I know it when I see it."

          —US Supreme Court Justice Potter Stewart

Things that I do:

  • Write C code and run it.
  • Use a hammer to drive nails.
  • Use an assembler to convert assembly to machine code.

Things that I ask others to do at my command (inspired by a nice capitalistic investment):

  • Write a piece of software.
  • Build me a deck.
  • Paint me a picture.

When I have ChatGPT create a painting, I don't feel like I painted it.

When I use the hammer, I don't feel like I'm asking the hammer to do something for me. Same with a C compiler or assembler.

And yet I just had Claude compile some C code right up there. Was that me writing C code and running it? Or was it asking something to write software for me? I have no doubt I could have prompted Claude with an English description of what I wanted and gotten the same output.

Is it because AIs seem human? You are asking them to do things in imprecise, human language. They respond in human ways and do the things you ask. And the relationship we are used to in those circumstances is more one of manager-report than one of colleague and peer. Whereas our relationship with a compiler is more similar to that of one with a hammer.

And yet... looking into that gray area makes me feel more confident that telling the AI to make something for me actually is an act of creation. What if I had an AI-powered hammer and asked it to hit a nail for me? What's the difference? But at that point, I can't say that I hammered it. It is important to me, this difference. Why?

By now, dear reader, I suspect we're both running out of steam and maybe we should leave it here to ponder.

I feel it's appropriate to conclude with some classic DNA:

"All I wanted to say," bellowed the computer, "is that my circuits are now irrevocably committed to calculating the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything." He paused and satisfied himself that he now had everyone's attention, before continuing more quietly. "But the program will take me a little while to run."

Fook glanced impatiently at his watch.

"How long?" he said.

"Seven and a half million years," said Deep Thought.

Lunkwill and Fook blinked at each other.

"Seven and a half million years!" they cried in chorus.

"Yes," declaimed Deep Thought, "I said I'd have to think about it, didn't I? And it occurs to me that running a program like this is bound to create an enormous amount of popular publicity for the whole area of philosophy in general. Everyone's going to have their own theories about what answer I'm eventually going to come up with, and who better to capitalize on that media market than you yourselves? So long as you can keep disagreeing with each other violently enough and maligning each other in the popular press, and so long as you have clever agents, you can keep yourselves on the gravy train for life. How does that sound?"

The two philosophers gaped at him.

"Bloody hell," said Majikthise, "now that is what I call thinking. Here, Vroomfondel, why do we never think of things like that?"

"Dunno," said Vroomfondel in an awed whisper; "think our brains must be too highly trained, Majikthise."

—The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, Douglas Adams

My Actual Contributions

As a post-script, here are my applied skills in the first part of this article.

Science fiction (honestly, the result was so vomitous I feel genuinely embarrassed having signed my name to it):

Write three paragraphs of sci-fi. Setting: We're in the middle of the novel at one of the pivotal fight sequences. The hero (choose a heroic sci-fi name) is leading two other refugees out under heavy laser fire from the baddies (choose an evil-sounding sci-fi name for this group). Our heroes have just been pinned down, but now see a way out.

What's the name of this novel?

Paintings and artwork:

Generate an image that shows the following: a woodcut, but with pastel coloring. Two wizardly computers hackers, one on the left of the frame and one on the right, facing each other, computers between them, both head down and focused. The left and right sides should be complementary and opposite colors to show that both sides are the same, yet different.

What's the name of this painting?

Ultima-likes:

I'm thinking of a game like Ultima I, except with a TUI. Visually, I was thinking of a 3x3 grid of characters per map cell. Some of them would be animated, e.g. the water on the ocean cells. And the map on the screen would be 7x7 map cells (or whatever Ultima I had)

It would be also like Ultima I in that it was fantasy.

The world should be procedurally-generated maybe with perlin noise. And the monsters should roam randomly, though they will be of prebuilt classes (no need to procedurally-generate monsters on the fly).

There should be some kind of DnD-ish stats, as well. No classes--just a general player is fine.

I'd like it in Rust using Ratatui. Color.

And I'd like the code well structured, with good separation between the UI and the game logic, as well as good code directory layout.

Carpentry:

Please replace my front deck.

Though I really did drive that nail into that piece of wood. I needed a picture to open with.

Uses of em dash within this piece are deliberate. Vim digraph: ^K-M.

" .section .text .globl main # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ # │ int fib(int n) n in: %edi / return: %eax │ # │ Stack frame: │ # │ %rbp-8 = saved %rbx (via push) │ # │ %rbp-12 = n (spilled so it survives both calls) │ # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ fib: pushq %rbp movq %rsp, %rbp pushq %rbx # callee-saved scratch subq $8, %rsp # 1 local (int n); keeps stack 16-byte aligned movl %edi, -12(%rbp) # spill n to stack cmpl $1, %edi jle .Lbase # n <= 1 → return n # --- fib(n-1) --- movl -12(%rbp), %edi subl $1, %edi call fib movl %eax, %ebx # %ebx = fib(n-1) # --- fib(n-2) --- movl -12(%rbp), %edi # reload n (call clobbered %edi) subl $2, %edi call fib # %eax = fib(n-2) addl %ebx, %eax # return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) jmp .Lret .Lbase: movl %edi, %eax # return n .Lret: addq $8, %rsp popq %rbx popq %rbp ret # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ # │ int main(void) │ # │ Stack frame: │ # │ %rbp-4 = int i (loop counter) │ # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ main: pushq %rbp movq %rsp, %rbp subq $16, %rsp # locals + alignment movl $0, -4(%rbp) # i = 0 .Lloop: cmpl $10, -4(%rbp) jge .Ldone # i >= 10 → exit # fib(i) movl -4(%rbp), %edi call fib # printf("%d: %d
", i, fib(i)) movl -4(%rbp), %esi # arg2 = i movl %eax, %edx # arg3 = fib(i) leaq fmt(%rip), %rdi # arg1 = fmt (RIP-relative) xorl %eax, %eax # AL=0: no XMM args to printf call printf addl $1, -4(%rbp) # i++ jmp .Lloop .Ldone: xorl %eax, %eax # return 0 leave ret OK, clanker, but does it work? % gcc fib.s -o fib -no-pie % ./fib 0: 0 1: 1 2: 1 3: 2 4: 3 5: 5 6: 8 7: 13 8: 21 9: 34 Well, holy golden ratios! It even used XOR to set exit status 0, the cheeky devil! So did I write that program? Yes. I feel I did. (I actually did hand-code the Fibonacci thing just now—I've done it a million times for classes.) I feel I wrote the program even though I didn't write the assembly, which is the program that actually runs! The LLM wrote that! Technically, it's the machine code that runs, but going from assembly to machine code now we're really getting into the land of strict, brainless, mathematically-precise translation, which I feel doesn't take away from the act of making any more than using a hammer to drive nails does. What's the real difference in that gray area? I'm not sure. I think it hinges on something that has to do with making versus asking to be made, even if that line isn't particularly clear. "I know it when I see it." —US Supreme Court Justice Potter Stewart Things that I do: Write C code and run it. Use a hammer to drive nails. Use an assembler to convert assembly to machine code. Things that I ask others to do at my command (inspired by a nice capitalistic investment): Write a piece of software. Build me a deck. Paint me a picture. When I have ChatGPT create a painting, I don't feel like I painted it. When I use the hammer, I don't feel like I'm asking the hammer to do something for me. Same with a C compiler or assembler. And yet I just had Claude compile some C code right up there. Was that me writing C code and running it? Or was it asking something to write software for me? I have no doubt I could have prompted Claude with an English description of what I wanted and gotten the same output. Is it because AIs seem human? You are asking them to do things in imprecise, human language. They respond in human ways and do the things you ask. And the relationship we are used to in those circumstances is more one of manager-report than one of colleague and peer. Whereas our relationship with a compiler is more similar to that of one with a hammer. And yet... looking into that gray area makes me feel more confident that telling the AI to make something for me actually is an act of creation. What if I had an AI-powered hammer and asked it to hit a nail for me? What's the difference? But at that point, I can't say that I hammered it. It is important to me, this difference. Why? By now, dear reader, I suspect we're both running out of steam and maybe we should leave it here to ponder. I feel it's appropriate to conclude with some classic DNA: "All I wanted to say," bellowed the computer, "is that my circuits are now irrevocably committed to calculating the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything." He paused and satisfied himself that he now had everyone's attention, before continuing more quietly. "But the program will take me a little while to run." Fook glanced impatiently at his watch. "How long?" he said. "Seven and a half million years," said Deep Thought. Lunkwill and Fook blinked at each other. "Seven and a half million years!" they cried in chorus. "Yes," declaimed Deep Thought, "I said I'd have to think about it, didn't I? And it occurs to me that running a program like this is bound to create an enormous amount of popular publicity for the whole area of philosophy in general. Everyone's going to have their own theories about what answer I'm eventually going to come up with, and who better to capitalize on that media market than you yourselves? So long as you can keep disagreeing with each other violently enough and maligning each other in the popular press, and so long as you have clever agents, you can keep yourselves on the gravy train for life. How does that sound?" The two philosophers gaped at him. "Bloody hell," said Majikthise, "now that is what I call thinking. Here, Vroomfondel, why do we never think of things like that?" "Dunno," said Vroomfondel in an awed whisper; "think our brains must be too highly trained, Majikthise." —The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, Douglas Adams My Actual Contributions As a post-script, here are my applied skills in the first part of this article. Science fiction (honestly, the result was so vomitous I feel genuinely embarrassed having signed my name to it): Write three paragraphs of sci-fi. Setting: We're in the middle of the novel at one of the pivotal fight sequences. The hero (choose a heroic sci-fi name) is leading two other refugees out under heavy laser fire from the baddies (choose an evil-sounding sci-fi name for this group). Our heroes have just been pinned down, but now see a way out. What's the name of this novel? Paintings and artwork: Generate an image that shows the following: a woodcut, but with pastel coloring. Two wizardly computers hackers, one on the left of the frame and one on the right, facing each other, computers between them, both head down and focused. The left and right sides should be complementary and opposite colors to show that both sides are the same, yet different. What's the name of this painting? Ultima-likes: I'm thinking of a game like Ultima I, except with a TUI. Visually, I was thinking of a 3x3 grid of characters per map cell. Some of them would be animated, e.g. the water on the ocean cells. And the map on the screen would be 7x7 map cells (or whatever Ultima I had) It would be also like Ultima I in that it was fantasy. The world should be procedurally-generated maybe with perlin noise. And the monsters should roam randomly, though they will be of prebuilt classes (no need to procedurally-generate monsters on the fly). There should be some kind of DnD-ish stats, as well. No classes--just a general player is fine. I'd like it in Rust using Ratatui. Color. And I'd like the code well structured, with good separation between the UI and the game logic, as well as good code directory layout. Carpentry: Please replace my front deck. Though I really did drive that nail into that piece of wood. I needed a picture to open with. Uses of em dash within this piece are deliberate. Vim digraph: ^K-M. Comments View Comments Reply

blocks.html_en:
Kael pressed his back against the shattered bulkhead, plasma scoring the air centimeters from his face. The Vorrkai assault drones had anticipated their route through the lower decks and now Rin was bleeding through her jacket sleeve and old Maret couldn't stop coughing from the vented coolant still hazing the corridor. Kael counted the pulse-intervals between shots. Three seconds. Maybe four. That was all the universe was offering him.

Then he saw it: the maintenance shaft behind the collapsed generator housing, its grate blown half-open by the same explosion that had caved in their original exit. It was tight. It was ugly. It ran directly over the Vorrkai's forward position, which was either the most dangerous path imaginable or the last one they'd ever think to watch. Kael grabbed Maret's collar and pointed without a word. The old man's eyes went wide, then hard. He nodded. Rin was already moving.

Kael came last, returning fire blind around the bulkhead corner, not to hit anything, just to make noise, and to give the drones something thermal to track while his people scrambled into the dark. A bolt caught the generator housing and the whole structure groaned, raining sparks down into the shaft on top of them. He hauled himself in, knees burning on the torn metal, and pulled the grate closed behind him with a sound he was certain every Vorrkai unit on the deck had heard. In the black ahead, Rin's hand found his wrist. Move, her grip said. Now. And so they did.

—Excerpt from The Vorrkai Interval, by Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall

sentences.zh: 然后他看到了：倒塌的发电机外壳后面的维护竖井，它的格栅被原来出口塌陷的同一场爆炸炸得半开。

sentences.en: It was tight.

sentences.en: It was ugly.

sentences.en: Kael grabbed Maret's collar and pointed without a word.
sentences.zh: 凯尔抓住马雷特的衣领，一言不发地指着。

sentences.en: The old man's eyes went wide, then hard.
sentences.zh: 老人的眼睛睁得大大的，然后变得严厉。

sentences.en: He nodded.
sentences.zh: 他点点头。

sentences.en: Rin was already moving.
sentences.zh: 凛已经动了。

sentences.en: Kael came last, returning fire blind around the bulkhead corner, not to hit anything, just to make noise, and to give the drones something thermal to track while his people scrambled into the dark.
sentences.zh: 凯尔最后来，他在舱壁拐角处盲目回击，不击中任何东西，只是制造噪音，并在他的人民爬入黑暗时为无人机提供热能跟踪。

sentences.en: A bolt caught the generator housing and the whole structure groaned, raining sparks down into the shaft on top of them.

sentences.zh: 在前方的黑暗中，凛的手找到了自己的手腕。

sentences.en: Now.
sentences.zh: 现在。

sentences.zh: 他们也确实这么做了。 ——摘自《The Vorrkai Interval》，作者：Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall

sentences.zh: 你打赌我涉足！

sentences.en: I rebuilt my front deck recently.
sentences.zh: 我最近重建了我的前甲板。

sentences.en: The old one was rotting out, so I grabbed a bunch of cedar and put it together.
sentences.zh: 旧的已经腐烂了，所以我抓起一束雪松并将其放在一起。

sentences.en: I'd been meaning to do it for a while, but couldn't find the time.
sentences.zh: 我一直想这样做有一段时间了，但找不到时间。

blocks.html_en: And, finally, here's some of the code I wrote for a TUI adventure roguelike:

sentences.en: And, finally, here's some of the code I wrote for a TUI adventure roguelike:
sentences.zh: 最后，这是我为 TUI 冒险 roguelike 编写的一些代码：

blocks.html_en: I'm an extremely prolific polymath, I'm sure you'd agree!

sentences.en: I'm an extremely prolific polymath, I'm sure you'd agree!
sentences.zh: 我是一位多产的博学者，我相信你会同意的！

blocks.html_en: I Am Uncomfortable

sentences.en: I Am Uncomfortable
sentences.zh: 我不舒服

blocks.html_en: I don't like lying. And yet I feel, dear reader, I have misled you. Yes, all that has been created (including my deck) and I was the initiator of all that creation. But I don't really feel like I made any of it. I'm uncomfortable claiming that I did so.

sentences.en: I don't like lying.
sentences.zh: 我不喜欢说谎。

sentences.en: And yet I feel, dear reader, I have misled you.
sentences.zh: 然而，亲爱的读者，我觉得我误导了你。

sentences.en: Yes, all that has been created (including my deck) and I was the initiator of all that creation.
sentences.zh: 是的，所有这些都已被创建（包括我的套牌），而我是所有这些创建的发起者。

sentences.en: But I don't really feel like I made any of it.
sentences.zh: 但我真的不觉得我做到了。

sentences.en: I'm uncomfortable claiming that I did so.
sentences.zh: 我对自己这样做感到不舒服。

blocks.html_en: Since you are certainly aware by now that all of the above is AI-generated (except my deck, which was created by skilled, paid craftsmen), perhaps you feel a little bit of discomfort with me claiming credit for doing those things, too.

sentences.en: Since you are certainly aware by now that all of the above is AI-generated (except my deck, which was created by skilled, paid craftsmen), perhaps you feel a little bit of discomfort with me claiming credit for doing those things, too.
sentences.zh: 既然你现在肯定知道以上所有内容都是人工智能生成的（除了我的套牌，它是由熟练的付费工匠创建的），也许你对我声称自己做这些事情也感到有点不舒服。

blocks.html_en: However, I don't think everyone feels this way. I know many people who ask contractors to build things and they phrase it like they built it.

sentences.en: However, I don't think everyone feels this way.
sentences.zh: 不过，我想并不是每个人都有这样的感觉。

sentences.en: I know many people who ask contractors to build things and they phrase it like they built it.
sentences.zh: 我知道很多人要求承包商建造东西，他们的措辞就像他们建造的一样。

blocks.html_en: "I put in a new front deck," they'd say, even though other people did all the work. Personally, I feel that's misleading. I'm more of a "I had a new front deck put in" kind of person.

sentences.en: "I put in a new front deck," they'd say, even though other people did all the work.
sentences.zh: “我安装了一个新的前甲板，”他们会说，尽管其他人做了所有的工作。

sentences.en: Personally, I feel that's misleading.
sentences.zh: 就我个人而言，我觉得这是一种误导。

sentences.en: I'm more of a "I had a new front deck put in" kind of person.
sentences.zh: 我更像是一个“我安装了新的前甲板”的人。

blocks.html_en: And when I do have Claude create something for me, I just can't say that I made it. Other people can, but I just can't. Again, I'm more prone to say, "I had this code built for me." I don't even feel comfortable MIT-licensing that (not-for-hire) work, if that's even legally possible. I just Unlicense it all.

sentences.en: And when I do have Claude create something for me, I just can't say that I made it.
sentences.zh: 当我确实让克劳德为我创造一些东西时，我只是不能说我做到了。

sentences.en: Other people can, but I just can't.
sentences.zh: 别人可以，但我就是不行。

sentences.en: Again, I'm more prone to say, "I had this code built for me."
sentences.zh: 同样，我更倾向于说：“我为我构建了这段代码。”

sentences.en: I don't even feel comfortable MIT-licensing that (not-for-hire) work, if that's even legally possible.
sentences.zh: 我什至不愿意让麻省理工学院许可（非雇佣）工作，即使这在法律上是可能的。

sentences.en: I just Unlicense it all.
sentences.zh: 我只是取消所有许可。

blocks.html_en: As a manager, I'd never say that I built a product. "My team built this," I'd say. And as a manager of LLMs: "My Agents built this."

sentences.en: As a manager, I'd never say that I built a product.
sentences.zh: 作为一名经理，我永远不会说我开发了一个产品。

sentences.en: "My team built this," I'd say.
sentences.zh: “我的团队建造了这个，”我会说。

sentences.en: And as a manager of LLMs: "My Agents built this."
sentences.zh: 作为法学硕士的经理：“我的代理人建造了这个。”

blocks.html_en: And that, for me, has very little weight in terms of making.

sentences.en: And that, for me, has very little weight in terms of making.
sentences.zh: 对我来说，这对制作来说影响很小。

blocks.html_en: I don't feel like I did anything. And I like doing things. I find pride in doing things.

sentences.en: I don't feel like I did anything.
sentences.zh: 我不觉得我做了什么。

sentences.en: And I like doing things.
sentences.zh: 而且我喜欢做事。

sentences.en: I find pride in doing things.
sentences.zh: 我对做事感到自豪。

blocks.html_en: Completing projects is great. I love completing projects. Capitalists love completing projects. Real artists ship.

sentences.en: Completing projects is great.
sentences.zh: 完成项目很棒。

sentences.en: I love completing projects.
sentences.zh: 我喜欢完成项目。

sentences.en: Capitalists love completing projects.
sentences.zh: 资本家喜欢完成项目。

sentences.en: Real artists ship.
sentences.zh: 真正的艺术家船。

blocks.html_en: But having others complete projects I initiated is entirely less fulfilling to me.

sentences.en: But having others complete projects I initiated is entirely less fulfilling to me.
sentences.zh: 但让其他人完成我发起的项目对我来说完全没有成就感。

blocks.html_en: It's not just the loss of the craft and the problem-solving challenge and whatever else. It's the loss of making.

sentences.en: It's not just the loss of the craft and the problem-solving challenge and whatever else.
sentences.zh: 这不仅仅是工艺的损失和解决问题的挑战等等。

sentences.en: It's the loss of making.
sentences.zh: 这是制造的损失。

blocks.html_en: What Did I Make Recently?

sentences.en: What Did I Make Recently?
sentences.zh: 我最近做了什么？

blocks.html_en: My wife wanted a no-frills flash card system for learning Spanish. "I just want a thing where I can put the words I want in a spreadsheet and then see it on flash cards." A prompt!

sentences.en: My wife wanted a no-frills flash card system for learning Spanish.
sentences.zh: 我的妻子想要一个简单的闪存卡系统来学习西班牙语。

sentences.en: "I just want a thing where I can put the words I want in a spreadsheet and then see it on flash cards."
sentences.zh: “我只想要一个可以将我想要的单词放入电子表格中，然后在闪存卡上看到它的东西。”

sentences.en: A prompt!
sentences.zh: 一个提示！

blocks.html_en: So I wrote it. By hand. I did use Claude to learn some basics, like the easiest way to get the data out of a Google Sheet (spoiler: it's the CSV endpoint), but I told it to generate no code.

sentences.en: So I wrote it.
sentences.zh: 所以我写了它。

sentences.en: By hand.
sentences.zh: 手工。

sentences.en: I did use Claude to learn some basics, like the easiest way to get the data out of a Google Sheet (spoiler: it's the CSV endpoint), but I told it to generate no code.
sentences.zh: 我确实使用 Claude 学习了一些基础知识，例如从 Google Sheet 中获取数据的最简单方法（剧透：它是 CSV 端点），但我告诉它不要生成任何代码。

blocks.html_en: Didn't take long. Only about 50x longer than it would have taken Claude to do it.

sentences.en: Didn't take long.
sentences.zh: 没花多长时间。

sentences.en: Only about 50x longer than it would have taken Claude to do it.
sentences.zh: 只比克劳德花费的时间长了大约 50 倍。

blocks.html_en: But I can put my name on that code and say that I made it. Was it a lot of code? No. Was it groundbreaking and amazing? Certainly not. But I'm infinitely more proud of that code than anything I've had Claude write, because I'm not capable of being proud of the latter.

sentences.en: But I can put my name on that code and say that I made it.
sentences.zh: 但我可以在代码上署上我的名字，并说我成功了。

sentences.en: Was it a lot of code?
sentences.zh: 代码很多吗？

sentences.en: No. Was it groundbreaking and amazing?
sentences.zh: 不。它具有开创性且令人惊叹吗？

sentences.en: Certainly not.
sentences.zh: 当然不是。

sentences.en: But I'm infinitely more proud of that code than anything I've had Claude write, because I'm not capable of being proud of the latter.
sentences.zh: 但我对这段代码比我让克劳德写的任何代码都感到无比自豪，因为我无法为后者感到自豪。

blocks.html_en: And my wife wouldn't go to her book club and say, "I wrote a flash card system to study Spanish." Admittedly, part of this would be because she didn't want to appear a geek, but mostly it's because it's untrue, even though she initiated the process.

sentences.en: And my wife wouldn't go to her book club and say, "I wrote a flash card system to study Spanish."
sentences.zh: 我的妻子不会去她的读书俱乐部说：“我写了一个抽认卡系统来学习西班牙语。”

sentences.en: Admittedly, part of this would be because she didn't want to appear a geek, but mostly it's because it's untrue, even though she initiated the process.
sentences.zh: 诚然，部分原因是她不想显得是个极客，但主要是因为这是不真实的，尽管她发起了这个过程。

blocks.html_en: What About The Art and Craft of Prompting?

sentences.en: What About The Art and Craft of Prompting?
sentences.zh: 提示的艺术和技巧怎么样？

blocks.html_en: After all, you create the prompts, don't you? You said you were proud of doing things. Isn't that doing a thing? And since so much got done, isn't it even more of doing a thing?

sentences.en: After all, you create the prompts, don't you?
sentences.zh: 毕竟，您创建了提示，不是吗？

sentences.en: You said you were proud of doing things.
sentences.zh: 你说你为做事感到自豪。

sentences.en: Isn't that doing a thing?
sentences.zh: 这不是做事吗？

sentences.en: And since so much got done, isn't it even more of doing a thing?
sentences.zh: 既然做了这么多，那不就更做了一件事吗？

blocks.html_en: I don't disagree. And I do agree that there is skill here in some important ways.

sentences.en: I don't disagree.
sentences.zh: 我不同意。

sentences.en: And I do agree that there is skill here in some important ways.
sentences.zh: 我确实同意，这在某些重要方面是有技巧的。

blocks.html_en: • You have to apply vision.

sentences.en: You have to apply vision.
sentences.zh: 你必须运用远见。

blocks.html_en: • You have to apply judgment.

sentences.en: You have to apply judgment.
sentences.zh: 你必须做出判断。

blocks.html_en: • You have to apply communication skill.

sentences.en: You have to apply communication skill.
sentences.zh: 你必须运用沟通技巧。

blocks.html_en: • You have to apply prompting skill.

sentences.en: You have to apply prompting skill.
sentences.zh: 你必须运用提示技巧。

blocks.html_en: Not all prompts are equally effective. Not all users of AI are as effective as one another. There's a very human contribution to be made here.

sentences.en: Not all prompts are equally effective.
sentences.zh: 并非所有提示都同样有效。

sentences.en: Not all users of AI are as effective as one another.
sentences.zh: 并非所有人工智能用户都彼此有效。

sentences.en: There's a very human contribution to be made here.
sentences.zh: 这里需要做出非常人性化的贡献。

blocks.html_en: But the skill is in effectively asking someone to make something for you.

sentences.en: But the skill is in effectively asking someone to make something for you.
sentences.zh: 但技巧在于有效地要求某人为你做一些事情。

blocks.html_en:
"Leadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it."

          —Dwight D. Eisenhower

sentences.en: "Leadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it." —Dwight D.
sentences.zh: “领导力是一门艺术，让别人做你想做的事情，因为他想做。” ——德怀特·D.

sentences.en: Eisenhower
sentences.zh: 艾森豪威尔

blocks.html_en: "Leadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it."

sentences.en: "Leadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it."
sentences.zh: “领导力是一门艺术，让别人做你想做的事情，因为他想做。”

sentences.en: —Dwight D.
sentences.zh: ——德怀特·D.

sentences.en: Eisenhower
sentences.zh: 艾森豪威尔

blocks.html_en: And I'm the kind of person who really misses the making of software. And prompting for software, to me, isn't the same as making the software. It's the same as asking someone else to make it.

sentences.en: And I'm the kind of person who really misses the making of software.
sentences.zh: 我是那种非常怀念软件制作过程的人。

sentences.en: And prompting for software, to me, isn't the same as making the software.
sentences.zh: 对我来说，提示软件与制作软件不同。

sentences.en: It's the same as asking someone else to make it.
sentences.zh: 这和要求别人做是一样的。

blocks.html_en: What About Compilers, Smartypants?

sentences.en: What About Compilers, Smartypants?
sentences.zh: 编译器、Smartypants 怎么样？

blocks.html_en: Isn't it just turtles all the way down?

sentences.en: Isn't it just turtles all the way down?
sentences.zh: 不就是一路下来的乌龟吗？

blocks.html_en: For any given C or Rust program, did I write that program? I wrote it in C. I didn't write any machine code. The computer wrote that.

sentences.en: For any given C or Rust program, did I write that program?
sentences.zh: 对于任何给定的 C 或 Rust 程序，我是否编写了该程序？

sentences.en: I wrote it in C.
sentences.zh: 我用C写的。

sentences.en: I didn't write any machine code.
sentences.zh: 我没有写任何机器代码。

sentences.en: The computer wrote that.
sentences.zh: 电脑是这样写的。

blocks.html_en: There's something different in my mind about that. I'm not sure if it's the mathematically-precise relationship between the C and the machine code...? And yet that's not spelled out. I'm relying on some hacker's deep knowledge of the CPU to generate that magnificent set of optimized instructions when I stupidly divide something by seven. And it's not deterministic, strictly, in that Clang and GCC emit different instructions. What about different platforms? The C is portable and the machine code is not. Did I write that program that's running on Windows even though I personally only ever built it on Linux?

sentences.en: There's something different in my mind about that.
sentences.zh: 我对此有一些不同的想法。

sentences.en: I'm not sure if it's the mathematically-precise relationship between the C and the machine code...?
sentences.zh: 我不确定 C 和机器代码之间是否存在数学上精确的关系......？

sentences.en: And yet that's not spelled out.
sentences.zh: 但这还没有明确说明。

sentences.en: I'm relying on some hacker's deep knowledge of the CPU to generate that magnificent set of optimized instructions when I stupidly divide something by seven.
sentences.zh: 当我愚蠢地将某个值除以七时，我依靠一些黑客对 CPU 的深入了解来生成一组出色的优化指令。

sentences.en: And it's not deterministic, strictly, in that Clang and GCC emit different instructions.
sentences.zh: 严格来说，它不是确定性的，因为 Clang 和 GCC 发出不同的指令。

sentences.en: What about different platforms?
sentences.zh: 不同平台又如何呢？

sentences.en: The C is portable and the machine code is not.
sentences.zh: C 语言是可移植的，而机器代码则不然。

sentences.en: Did I write that program that's running on Windows even though I personally only ever built it on Linux?
sentences.zh: 我是否编写了在 Windows 上运行的程序，尽管我个人只在 Linux 上构建过它？

blocks.html_en: Going further in the murky gray, what if I instruct an LLM to be a C compiler and emit assembly code for some platform?

sentences.en: Going further in the murky gray, what if I instruct an LLM to be a C compiler and emit assembly code for some platform?
sentences.zh: 更进一步，如果我指示 LLM 成为 C 编译器并为某些平台发出汇编代码会怎么样？

blocks.html_en: Let's try:

sentences.en: Let's try:
sentences.zh: 让我们尝试一下：

blocks.html_en: Claude says:

sentences.en: Claude says:
sentences.zh: 克劳德 说：

blocks.html_en: OK, clanker, but does it work?

sentences.en: OK, clanker, but does it work?
sentences.zh: 好吧，叮叮当当，但这有用吗？

blocks.html_en: Well, holy golden ratios! It even used XOR to set exit status 0, the cheeky devil!

sentences.en: Well, holy golden ratios!
sentences.zh: 好吧，神圣的黄金比例！

sentences.en: It even used XOR to set exit status 0, the cheeky devil!
sentences.zh: 它甚至使用 XOR 将退出状态设置为 0，厚颜无耻的魔鬼！

blocks.html_en: So did I write that program? Yes. I feel I did. (I actually did hand-code the Fibonacci thing just now—I've done it a million times for classes.) I feel I wrote the program even though I didn't write the assembly, which is the program that actually runs! The LLM wrote that!

sentences.en: So did I write that program?
sentences.zh: 那么那个程序是我写的吗？

sentences.en: Yes.
sentences.zh: 是的。

sentences.en: I feel I did.
sentences.zh: 我感觉我做到了。

sentences.en: (I actually did hand-code the Fibonacci thing just now—I've done it a million times for classes.)
sentences.zh: （事实上​​，我刚才已经手工编写了斐波那契数列——我已经在课堂上编写了一百万次了。）

sentences.en: I feel I wrote the program even though I didn't write the assembly, which is the program that actually runs!
sentences.zh: 我感觉我写了程序，尽管我没有写程序集，而程序集才是实际运行的程序！

sentences.en: The LLM wrote that!
sentences.zh: LLM 写的！

blocks.html_en: Technically, it's the machine code that runs, but going from assembly to machine code now we're really getting into the land of strict, brainless, mathematically-precise translation, which I feel doesn't take away from the act of making any more than using a hammer to drive nails does.

sentences.en: Technically, it's the machine code that runs, but going from assembly to machine code now we're really getting into the land of strict, brainless, mathematically-precise translation, which I feel doesn't take away from the act of making any more than using a hammer to drive nails does.
sentences.zh: 从技术上讲，是机器代码在运行，但从汇编到机器代码，现在我们真的进入了严格的、无脑的、数学上精确的翻译领域，我觉得这并没有影响制造行为，就像用锤子钉钉子一样。

blocks.html_en: What's the real difference in that gray area? I'm not sure. I think it hinges on something that has to do with making versus asking to be made, even if that line isn't particularly clear.

sentences.en: What's the real difference in that gray area?
sentences.zh: 这个灰色地带的真正区别是什么？

sentences.en: I'm not sure.
sentences.zh: 我不知道。

sentences.en: I think it hinges on something that has to do with making versus asking to be made, even if that line isn't particularly clear.
sentences.zh: 我认为这取决于与制作和要求制作有关的东西，即使这条线不是特别明确。

blocks.html_en:
"I know it when I see it."

          —US Supreme Court Justice Potter Stewart

sentences.en: "I know it when I see it." —US Supreme Court Justice Potter Stewart
sentences.zh: “我一看到就知道了。” ——美国最高法院法官波特·斯图尔特

blocks.html_en: "I know it when I see it."

sentences.en: "I know it when I see it."
sentences.zh: “我一看到就知道了。”

sentences.en: —US Supreme Court Justice Potter Stewart
sentences.zh: ——美国最高法院法官波特·斯图尔特

blocks.html_en: Things that I do:

sentences.en: Things that I do:
sentences.zh: 我所做的事情：

blocks.html_en: • Write C code and run it.

sentences.en: Write C code and run it.
sentences.zh: 编写 C 代码并运行它。

blocks.html_en: • Use a hammer to drive nails.

sentences.en: Use a hammer to drive nails.
sentences.zh: 使用锤子来钉钉子。

blocks.html_en: • Use an assembler to convert assembly to machine code.

sentences.en: Use an assembler to convert assembly to machine code.
sentences.zh: 使用汇编器将汇编代码转换为机器代码。

blocks.html_en: Things that I ask others to do at my command (inspired by a nice capitalistic investment):

sentences.en: Things that I ask others to do at my command (inspired by a nice capitalistic investment):
sentences.zh: 我要求别人在我的指挥下做的事情（受到良好的资本主义投资的启发）：

blocks.html_en: • Write a piece of software.

sentences.en: Write a piece of software.
sentences.zh: 写一个软件。

blocks.html_en: • Build me a deck.

sentences.en: Build me a deck.
sentences.zh: 给我建一个套牌。

blocks.html_en: • Paint me a picture.

sentences.en: Paint me a picture.
sentences.zh: 给我画一幅画。

blocks.html_en: When I have ChatGPT create a painting, I don't feel like I painted it.

sentences.en: When I have ChatGPT create a painting, I don't feel like I painted it.
sentences.zh: 当我使用 ChatGPT 创作一幅画时，我不觉得它是我画的。

blocks.html_en: When I use the hammer, I don't feel like I'm asking the hammer to do something for me. Same with a C compiler or assembler.

sentences.en: When I use the hammer, I don't feel like I'm asking the hammer to do something for me.
sentences.zh: 当我使用锤子时，我并不觉得我在要求锤子为我做某事。

sentences.en: Same with a C compiler or assembler.
sentences.zh: 与 C 编译器或汇编器相同。

blocks.html_en: And yet I just had Claude compile some C code right up there. Was that me writing C code and running it? Or was it asking something to write software for me? I have no doubt I could have prompted Claude with an English description of what I wanted and gotten the same output.

sentences.en: And yet I just had Claude compile some C code right up there.
sentences.zh: 但我只是让 Claude 在那里编译了一些 C 代码。

sentences.en: Was that me writing C code and running it?

blocks.html_en: Is it because AIs seem human? You are asking them to do things in imprecise, human language. They respond in human ways and do the things you ask. And the relationship we are used to in those circumstances is more one of manager-report than one of colleague and peer. Whereas our relationship with a compiler is more similar to that of one with a hammer.

blocks.html_en: I feel it's appropriate to conclude with some classic DNA:

sentences.en: I feel it's appropriate to conclude with some classic DNA:
sentences.zh: 我觉得用一些经典的 DNA 来总结是合适的：

blocks.html_en:
"All I wanted to say," bellowed the computer, "is that my circuits are now irrevocably committed to calculating the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything." He paused and satisfied himself that he now had everyone's attention, before continuing more quietly. "But the program will take me a little while to run."

Fook glanced impatiently at his watch.

"How long?" he said.

"Seven and a half million years," said Deep Thought.

Lunkwill and Fook blinked at each other.

"Seven and a half million years!" they cried in chorus.

"Yes," declaimed Deep Thought, "I said I'd have to think about it, didn't I? And it occurs to me that running a program like this is bound to create an enormous amount of popular publicity for the whole area of philosophy in general. Everyone's going to have their own theories about what answer I'm eventually going to come up with, and who better to capitalize on that media market than you yourselves? So long as you can keep disagreeing with each other violently enough and maligning each other in the popular press, and so long as you have clever agents, you can keep yourselves on the gravy train for life. How does that sound?"

The two philosophers gaped at him.

"Bloody hell," said Majikthise, "now that is what I call thinking. Here, Vroomfondel, why do we never think of things like that?"

"Dunno," said Vroomfondel in an awed whisper; "think our brains must be too highly trained, Majikthise."

—The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, Douglas Adams

blocks.html_en:
I'm thinking of a game like Ultima I, except with a TUI. Visually, I was thinking of a 3x3 grid of characters per map cell. Some of them would be animated, e.g. the water on the ocean cells. And the map on the screen would be 7x7 map cells (or whatever Ultima I had)

It would be also like Ultima I in that it was fantasy.

The world should be procedurally-generated maybe with perlin noise. And the monsters should roam randomly, though they will be of prebuilt classes (no need to procedurally-generate monsters on the fly).

There should be some kind of DnD-ish stats, as well. No classes--just a general player is fine.

I'd like it in Rust using Ratatui. Color.

And I'd like the code well structured, with good separation between the UI and the game logic, as well as good code directory layout.

2026-03-12

我做了这个！

TLDR：我通过制作东西获得了很多满足感。 我不认为别人应我的要求建造的东西是我自己做的，因此不太有成就感。 然后我感到悲伤。 这篇文章开头很精彩，然后就陷入了困境。

有很多关于我称之为“人工智能开发分裂”的文章，我认为这些文章有很多道理：

  • 手艺的丧失，手工编码
  • 丧失低级问题解决能力
  • 失去乐趣
  • 获得高水平问题解决能力
  • 完成搁置的项目
  • 获得乐趣

我们只会承认这些对于不同的开发人员来说是正确的。 但还有一件事让我烦恼。

在我们开始之前，请先了解背景故事，以便您可以更好地了解我的观点：

  • 我是 X 代黑客；我在 80 年代的微电脑时代开始接触。
  • 我拥有计算机科学学士和硕士学位。
  • 我有 20 年的行业经验（惠普、初创公司、联合创始人、动视等）。
  • 过去 9 年担任 CS 讲师，目前在俄勒冈州立大学卡斯卡德分校。
  • 在 AI-乌托邦/末日等级上，我是 65% 的末日。
  • 我有时是克劳德代码的用户。
  • 我有时用手编码。
  • 我父亲在一所社区大学教授哲学 35 年。 这可能有助于解释本博客文章的后半部分。

在本文中，我将使用“AI”来表示“生成式人工智能和法学硕士”。 抱歉，经历过这么多人工智能冬天的老兵们。

插曲！

在开始之前，我想与大家分享一些我最新的科幻小说。 你们中有些人可能不知道，除了 Beej's Guides 之外，我还写科幻小说。

凯尔将背靠在破碎的舱壁上，等离子体划破了他脸上几厘米的空气。 沃凯攻击无人机已经预料到了它们穿过下层甲板的路线，现在琳的夹克袖子里正在流血，老马雷特无法停止咳嗽，因为排出的冷却剂仍然笼罩着走廊。 凯尔计算了射击之间的脉冲间隔。 三秒钟。 也许四个。 这就是宇宙为他提供的一切。 然后他看到了：倒塌的发电机外壳后面的维护竖井，它的格栅被原来出口塌陷的同一场爆炸炸得半开。 很紧。 太丑了。 它直接越过沃凯号的前沿位置，这要么是可以想象到的最危险的路径，要么是他们最不想看到的路径。 凯尔抓住马雷特的衣领，一言不发地指着。 老人的眼睛睁得大大的，然后变得严厉。 他点点头。 凛已经动了。 凯尔最后来，他在舱壁拐角处盲目回击，不击中任何东西，只是制造噪音，并在他的人民爬入黑暗时为无人机提供热能跟踪。 一颗螺栓卡住了发电机外壳，整个结构发出呻吟声，火花如雨点般落入其顶部的轴中。 他把自己拖了进去，膝盖在撕裂的金属上燃烧，然后拉上了身后的格栅，他确信甲板上的每个沃凯单位都听到了声音。 在前方的黑暗中，凛的手找到了自己的手腕。 移动，她的握力说。 现在。 他们也确实这么做了。 ——摘自《The Vorrkai Interval》，作者：Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall

而且，在我现在充裕的业余时间里，我创作艺术！ 这是一幅木刻，以粉彩绘制，展示了我最喜欢的一些主题。

Brian "Beej Jorgensen" Hall 的《机器之镜》，1300 美元。

木工？ 你打赌我涉足！ 我最近重建了我的前甲板。 旧的已经腐烂了，所以我抓起一束雪松并将其放在一起。 我一直想这样做有一段时间了，但找不到时间。

最后，这是我为 TUI 冒险 roguelike 编写的一些代码：

    fn try_move(&mut self, dx: i32, dy: i32) {
        let nx = self.player.x + dx;
        let ny = self.player.y + dy;

        // Check for monster combat
        if let Some(idx) = self.world.monster_at(nx, ny) {
            let result = {
                let monster = &mut self.world.monsters[idx];
                resolve_combat(&mut self.player, monster, &mut self.rng)
            };

            self.messages.push_many(result.messages);

            if result.monster_defeated {
                let monster = &self.world.monsters[idx];
                let xp = monster.xp_reward;
                let gold = monster.gold_reward;
                self.player.xp += xp;
                self.player.gold += gold;
                if gold > 0 {
                    self.messages.push(format!("You find {} gold!", gold));
                }
                if self.player.try_level_up() {
                    self.messages.push(format!(
                        "Level up! You are now level {}!",
                        self.player.level
                    ));
                }
            }

            if !self.player.is_alive() {
                self.messages.push("You have been slain! Rest in peace...");
            }

            self.advance_turn();
            return;
        }

        // Check terrain passability
        if self.world.is_passable(nx, ny) {
            self.player.x = nx;
            self.player.y = ny;
            self.advance_turn();
        } else {
            let terrain = self.world.terrain_at(nx, ny);
            self.messages.push(format!("The {} blocks your path.", terrain.name()));
        }
    }

我是一位多产的博学者，我相信你会同意的！

我不舒服

我不喜欢说谎。 然而，亲爱的读者，我觉得我误导了你。 是的，所有这些都已被创建（包括我的套牌），而我是所有这些创建的发起者。 但我真的不觉得我做到了。 我对自己这样做感到不舒服。

既然你现在肯定知道以上所有内容都是人工智能生成的（除了我的套牌，它是由熟练的付费工匠创建的），也许你对我声称自己做这些事情也感到有点不舒服。

不过，我想并不是每个人都有这样的感觉。 我知道很多人要求承包商建造东西，他们的措辞就像他们建造的一样。

“我安装了一个新的前甲板，”他们会说，尽管其他人做了所有的工作。 就我个人而言，我觉得这是一种误导。 我更像是一个“我安装了新的前甲板”的人。

当我确实让克劳德为我创造一些东西时，我只是不能说我做到了。 别人可以，但我就是不行。 同样，我更倾向于说：“我为我构建了这段代码。” 我什至不愿意让麻省理工学院许可（非雇佣）工作，即使这在法律上是可能的。 我只是取消所有许可。

作为一名经理，我永远不会说我开发了一个产品。 “我的团队建造了这个，”我会说。 作为法学硕士的经理：“我的代理人建造了这个。”

对我来说，这对制作来说影响很小。

我不觉得我做了什么。 而且我喜欢做事。 我对做事感到自豪。

完成项目很棒。 我喜欢完成项目。 资本家喜欢完成项目。 真正的艺术家船。

但让其他人完成我发起的项目对我来说完全没有成就感。

这不仅仅是工艺的损失和解决问题的挑战等等。 这是制造的损失。

我最近做了什么？

我的妻子想要一个简单的闪存卡系统来学习西班牙语。 “我只想要一个可以将我想要的单词放入电子表格中，然后在闪存卡上看到它的东西。” 一个提示！

所以我写了它。 手工。 我确实使用 Claude 学习了一些基础知识，例如从 Google Sheet 中获取数据的最简单方法（剧透：它是 CSV 端点），但我告诉它不要生成任何代码。

--------------------------------------------
Language                     files      code
--------------------------------------------
JavaScript                       2       112
CSS                              1        33
HTML                             1        32
--------------------------------------------
SUM:                             4       177
--------------------------------------------

没花多长时间。 只比克劳德花费的时间长了大约 50 倍。

但我可以在代码上署上我的名字，并说我成功了。 代码很多吗？ 不。它具有开创性且令人惊叹吗？ 当然不是。 但我对这段代码比我让克劳德写的任何代码都感到无比自豪，因为我无法为后者感到自豪。

我的妻子不会去她的读书俱乐部说：“我写了一个抽认卡系统来学习西班牙语。” 诚然，部分原因是她不想显得是个极客，但主要是因为这是不真实的，尽管她发起了这个过程。

提示的艺术和技巧怎么样？

毕竟，您创建了提示，不是吗？ 你说你为做事感到自豪。 这不是做事吗？ 既然做了这么多，那不就更做了一件事吗？

我不同意。 我确实同意，这在某些重要方面是有技巧的。

  • 你必须运用远见。
  • 你必须做出判断。
  • 你必须运用沟通技巧。
  • 你必须运用提示技巧。

并非所有提示都同样有效。 并非所有人工智能用户都彼此有效。 这里需要做出非常人性化的贡献。

但技巧在于有效地要求某人为你做一些事情。

“领导力是一门艺术，让别人做你想做的事情，因为他想做。” ——德怀特·D. 艾森豪威尔

我是那种非常怀念软件制作过程的人。 对我来说，提示软件与制作软件不同。 这和要求别人做是一样的。

编译器、Smartypants 怎么样？

不就是一路下来的乌龟吗？

对于任何给定的 C 或 Rust 程序，我是否编写了该程序？ 我用C写的。 我没有写任何机器代码。 电脑是这样写的。

我对此有一些不同的想法。 我不确定 C 和机器代码之间是否存在数学上精确的关系......？ 但这还没有明确说明。 当我愚蠢地将某个值除以七时，我依靠一些黑客对 CPU 的深入了解来生成一组出色的优化指令。 严格来说，它不是确定性的，因为 Clang 和 GCC 发出不同的指令。 不同平台又如何呢？ C 语言是可移植的，而机器代码则不然。 我是否编写了在 Windows 上运行的程序，尽管我个人只在 Linux 上构建过它？

更进一步，如果我指示 LLM 成为 C 编译器并为某些平台发出汇编代码会怎么样？

让我们尝试一下：

Convert this C program into x86_64 assembly for Linux.

#include <stdio.h>

int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}

int main(void)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)
        printf("%d: %d\n", i, fib(i));
}

克劳德 说：

# fib.s - Recursive Fibonacci, x86_64 Linux, AT&T syntax
# Build: gcc fib.s -o fib -no-pie

    .section .rodata
fmt:
    .string "%d: %d\n"

    .section .text
    .globl main

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  int fib(int n)          n in: %edi / return: %eax  │
# │  Stack frame:                                        │
# │    %rbp-8  = saved %rbx  (via push)                 │
# │    %rbp-12 = n  (spilled so it survives both calls)  │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
fib:
    pushq   %rbp
    movq    %rsp, %rbp
    pushq   %rbx                # callee-saved scratch
    subq    $8, %rsp            # 1 local (int n); keeps stack 16-byte aligned

    movl    %edi, -12(%rbp)     # spill n to stack

    cmpl    $1, %edi
    jle     .Lbase              # n <= 1 → return n

    # --- fib(n-1) ---
    movl    -12(%rbp), %edi
    subl    $1, %edi
    call    fib
    movl    %eax, %ebx          # %ebx = fib(n-1)

    # --- fib(n-2) ---
    movl    -12(%rbp), %edi     # reload n (call clobbered %edi)
    subl    $2, %edi
    call    fib                 # %eax = fib(n-2)

    addl    %ebx, %eax          # return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
    jmp     .Lret

.Lbase:
    movl    %edi, %eax          # return n

.Lret:
    addq    $8, %rsp
    popq    %rbx
    popq    %rbp
    ret

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  int main(void)                                      │
# │  Stack frame:                                        │
# │    %rbp-4  = int i  (loop counter)                  │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
main:
    pushq   %rbp
    movq    %rsp, %rbp
    subq    $16, %rsp           # locals + alignment

    movl    $0, -4(%rbp)        # i = 0

.Lloop:
    cmpl    $10, -4(%rbp)
    jge     .Ldone              # i >= 10 → exit

    # fib(i)
    movl    -4(%rbp), %edi
    call    fib

    # printf("%d: %d\n", i, fib(i))
    movl    -4(%rbp), %esi      # arg2 = i
    movl    %eax, %edx          # arg3 = fib(i)
    leaq    fmt(%rip), %rdi     # arg1 = fmt  (RIP-relative)
    xorl    %eax, %eax          # AL=0: no XMM args to printf
    call    printf

    addl    $1, -4(%rbp)        # i++
    jmp     .Lloop

.Ldone:
    xorl    %eax, %eax          # return 0
    leave
    ret

好吧，叮叮当当，但这有用吗？

% gcc fib.s -o fib -no-pie
% ./fib
0: 0
1: 1
2: 1
3: 2
4: 3
5: 5
6: 8
7: 13
8: 21
9: 34

好吧，神圣的黄金比例！ 它甚至使用 XOR 将退出状态设置为 0，厚颜无耻的魔鬼！

那么那个程序是我写的吗？ 是的。 我感觉我做到了。 （事实上​​，我刚才已经手工编写了斐波那契数列——我已经在课堂上编写了一百万次了。） 我感觉我写了程序，尽管我没有写程序集，而程序集才是实际运行的程序！ LLM 写的！

从技术上讲，是机器代码在运行，但从汇编到机器代码，现在我们真的进入了严格的、无脑的、数学上精确的翻译领域，我觉得这并没有影响制造行为，就像用锤子钉钉子一样。

这个灰色地带的真正区别是什么？ 我不知道。 我认为这取决于与制作和要求制作有关的东西，即使这条线不是特别明确。

“我一看到就知道了。” ——美国最高法院法官波特·斯图尔特

我所做的事情：

  • 编写 C 代码并运行它。
  • 使用锤子来钉钉子。
  • 使用汇编器将汇编代码转换为机器代码。

我要求别人在我的指挥下做的事情（受到良好的资本主义投资的启发）：

  • 写一个软件。
  • 给我建一个套牌。
  • 给我画一幅画。

当我使用 ChatGPT 创作一幅画时，我不觉得它是我画的。

当我使用锤子时，我并不觉得我在要求锤子为我做某事。 与 C 编译器或汇编器相同。

但我只是让 Claude 在那里编译了一些 C 代码。 那是我编写并运行 C 代码吗？ 或者是要求为我编写软件？ 我毫不怀疑我可以向克劳德提示我想要什么的英文描述并得到相同的输出。

是因为人工智能看起来像人类吗？ 你要求他们用不精确的人类语言做事。 他们以人性化的方式回应并做你要求的事情。 在这种情况下，我们习惯的关系更多地是一种经理报告关系，而不是同事和同事之间的关系。 而我们与编译器的关系更像是与锤子的关系。

然而…… 研究这个灰色区域让我更有信心告诉人工智能为我做一些东西实际上是一种创造行为。 如果我有一把人工智能锤子并让它帮我敲钉子怎么办？ 有什么区别？ 但在这一点上，我不能说我锤了它。 这种差异对我来说很重要。 为什么？

到目前为止，亲爱的读者，我怀疑我们都已经失去了动力，也许我们应该把它留在这里思考。

我觉得用一些经典的 DNA 来总结是合适的：

“我想说的是，”计算机咆哮道，“我的电路现在不可撤销地致力于计算生命、宇宙和一切终极问题的答案。” 他停顿了一下，确信自己现在已经引起了所有人的注意，然后更加安静地继续说道。 “但是这个程序需要我花一点时间才能运行。” 福克不耐烦地看了一眼手表。 “多久？” 他说。 “七百五十万年了。”深思说道。 朗克威尔和福克互相眨了眨眼。 “七百五十万年！” 他们异口同声地哭了起来。 “是的，”深思大声说道，“我说过我必须考虑一下，不是吗？ 我突然想到，运行这样一个项目必然会为整个哲学领域带来大量的公众宣传。 每个人都会对我最终会得出什么答案有自己的理论，还有谁比你们自己更能利用这个媒体市场呢？ 只要你们能继续在大众媒体上激烈地表达不同意见并互相诽谤，只要你们有聪明的代理人，你们就可以让自己一辈子都在顺风顺水的火车上。 听起来怎么样？” 两位哲学家目瞪口呆地看着他。 “见鬼，”马吉克蒂斯说，“这就是我所说的思考。 在这里，Vroomfondel，为什么我们从来没有想到这样的事情？” “不知道，”卢姆方德尔敬畏地低声说道。 “我想我们的大脑一定是训练有素的，Majikthise。” ——《银河系漫游指南》，道格拉斯·亚当斯

我的实际贡献

作为后记，以下是我在本文第一部分中应用的技能。

科幻小说（说实话，结果太令人作呕了，我在上面签下自己的名字真的感到很尴尬）：
